--- SOURCE 1: Policy and Action - Community Involved In Sustaining Agriculture ---
URL: https://www.buylocalfood.org/get-involved/policy-and-action/

SUMMARY:
We work with local, state, and national partners to support policies that work for New England farms and communities.

FULL CONTENT:
[CISA – Community Involved In Sustaining Agriculture](https://www.buylocalfood.org/)

![page banner](https://www.buylocalfood.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/GreenhouseSproutsRiverland-e1641502608701_660x135_acf_cropped.jpg)

# Policy and Action

![](https://www.buylocalfood.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Agday-e1568746590653-600x199.jpg)

Staff of CISA and other MA food nonprofits meet with Rep Smitty Pignatelli during Ag Day.

Staff of CISA and other MA food nonprofits meet with Rep Smitty Pignatelli during Ag Day.

### \_

### Select a page from the menu to the right to read more about CISA’s current activities and priorities.

At CISA, we believe that public policy can play an important role in supporting local agriculture. We work with local, state, and national partners to support policies that work for New England farms and communities. Like CISA’s [Facebook page](http://www.facebook.com/buylocalfood.org "http://www.facebook.com/buylocalfood.org") and/or sign up for our [monthly email newsletter](http://visitor.constantcontact.com/manage/optin?v=001oGSL6wu1SRZ9RnEgl1kRIfRm5z5omPyx "http://visitor.constantcontact.com/manage/optin?v=001oGSL6wu1SRZ9RnEgl1kRIfRm5z5omPyx") to keep informed of state and local policy actions and select national alerts.

Want to reach out to your state legislators but aren’t sure who they are? [You can find out here](https://malegislature.gov/Search/FindMyLegislator).

## Partner Organizations and Policy Resources:

**[American Farmland Trust (AFT)](http://www.farmland.org/)**  
AFT is an organization dedicated to the conservation of farm and ranch land. They are focused on sustainable farming, healthy environmental practices and lasting rural landscapes.

**[Center for Rural Affairs](http://www.cfra.org/)**  
The Center for Rural Affairs advocates for social, environmental, and economic issues that affect rural America. Policy focused on: Rural Development in the Farm Bill; Microenterprise; Rural Health Policy; Livestock Markets, and; Clean Energy.

**[National Sustainable Agriculture Coalition (NSAC)](http://sustainableagriculture.net/)**  
NSAC advocates for federal policy reform to advance the sustainability of agriculture, food systems, natural resources, and rural communities. NSAC also publishes in depth analyses of the Farm Bill, Conservation, Local and Regional Food Systems, and Food Safety.

**[National Young Farmers Coalition](https://www.youngfarmers.org/)**

The National Young Farmers Coalition aims to shift power and change policy to equitably resource our new generation of working farmers, supporting a brighter future for U.S. Agriculture.

**[New England Farmers Union (NEFU)](http://newenglandfarmersunion.org/)**  
NEFU is the New England chapter of the National Farmers Union. They are a member-driven organization and advocate for federal policy that will benefit family farmers, fishermen, nurserymen, and cooperatives.

[Northeast Organic Farming Association/ Massachusetts (NOFA/Mass)](http://www.nofamass.org/)NOFA/Mass advocates for sustainable agricultural policies that strengthen the resilience of our local communities. While primarily an educational organization, our policy team works on issues as diverse as organic standards, food system transparency, and reducing burdensome regulations on farmers.

[**Northeast Sustainable Agriculture Working Group (NESAWG)**](https://nesawg.org/)  
NESAWG is the network maintains an overview of the region’s movement on the local, state and regional level towards a more sustainable food system and guides their network members in advocacy to maximize their collective impact.

*If you would like to volunteer to work on policy issues, contact [Kelly Coleman](mailto:kelly@buylocalfood.org), Deputy Director, at 413-665-7100, ext. 14.*

**[New England Farmers Union (NEFU)](http://newenglandfarmersunion.org/)**  
NEFU is the New England chapter of the National Farmers Union. They are a member-driven organization and advocate for federal policy that will benefit family farmers, fishermen, nurserymen, and cooperatives.

[Northeast Organic Farming Association/ Massachusetts (NOFA/Mass)](http://www.nofamass.org/)NOFA/Mass advocates for sustainable agricultural policies that strengthen the resilience of our local communities. While primarily an educational organization, our policy team works on issues as diverse as organic standards, food system transparency, and reducing burdensome regulations on farmers.

[**Northeast Sustainable Agriculture Working Group (NESAWG)**](https://nesawg.org/)  
NESAWG is the network maintains an overview of the region’s movement on the local, state and regional level towards a more sustainable food system and guides their network members in advocacy to maximize their collective impact.

*If you would like to volunteer to work on policy issues, contact [Kelly Coleman](mailto:kelly@buylocalfood.org), Deputy Director, at 413-665-7100, ext. 14.*

--- SOURCE 2: How Farmers Can Participate in Agricultural Policy Processes ---
URL: https://agriculture.institute/agricultural-policy/farmers-participate-agricultural-policy

SUMMARY:
Effective agricultural policy participation requires bringing together diverse voices that represent the full spectrum of agricultural

FULL CONTENT:
![Agriculture Notes by Agriculture.Institute](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAysAAABoAQAAAADZCFKuAAAAAnRSTlMAAHaTzTgAAAAhSURBVGje7cExAQAAAMKg9U9tDQ+gAAAAAAAAAAAAAHgwKdgAAeuqigkAAAAASUVORK5CYII=)
![Agriculture Notes by Agriculture.Institute](https://agriculture.institute/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Agriculture.Institute-Navbar-Logo.jpeg)

# How Farmers Can Participate in Agricultural Policy Processes

Nov 20, 2023

Picture this: a small-scale rice farmer in rural Thailand sits at a government meeting, sharing her struggles with water shortages and market access. Her voice, once unheard, now shapes national agricultural policy. This scene represents a growing movement worldwide where farmers and agricultural stakeholders are no longer passive recipients of top-down policies, but active participants in creating the rules that govern their livelihoods. Participation in agricultural policy processes ensures that the people who know farming best—the farmers themselves—have a meaningful say in the decisions that affect their daily lives and economic futures.

Table of Contents

## What does participation in agricultural policy really mean?

Agricultural policy participation goes far beyond attending a single meeting or filling out a survey. It’s a comprehensive approach where farmers, cooperatives, agricultural businesses, researchers, and community organizations actively engage in shaping policies from conception to evaluation. Think of it as a collaborative conversation rather than a one-way lecture.

This participation can take many forms, from informal consultations in village squares to formal representation on national agricultural boards. The key is that stakeholders don’t just receive information—they contribute knowledge, express concerns, propose solutions, and help monitor whether policies actually work in practice.

Consider how different this is from traditional policy-making, where government officials might design agricultural programs in air-conditioned offices, far removed from the realities of drought-stricken fields or flooded farmlands. Participatory approaches bridge this gap by bringing real-world agricultural experience directly into policy discussions.

## The four stages where your voice matters most

### Policy formation: planting the seeds of change

The formation stage is where policies are born, and it’s arguably the most crucial point for stakeholder input. During this phase, farmers and agricultural groups can help identify problems, suggest solutions, and ensure that proposed policies address real needs rather than perceived ones.

For example, when Kenya was developing its National Food and Nutrition Security Policy, farmers’ organizations participated in nationwide consultations. They shared firsthand experiences about challenges like unpredictable weather patterns, limited access to quality seeds, and market volatility. This input helped shape a policy that addressed practical concerns rather than theoretical problems.

Participation at this stage often involves focus group discussions, public hearings, stakeholder workshops, and collaborative research initiatives. The goal is to ensure that policies are grounded in reality and have the support of those who will be most affected by them.

### Implementation: turning plans into action

Even the best-designed policy can fail if implementation doesn’t match the original vision. This is where continued stakeholder participation becomes essential. Farmers and agricultural organizations can serve as implementation partners, helping to deliver programs, provide feedback on early challenges, and suggest modifications.

In Brazil’s Family Agriculture Program (PRONAF), for instance, farmer cooperatives don’t just receive credit—they actively participate in program delivery. They help identify eligible farmers, provide technical assistance, and ensure that credit reaches those who need it most. This participatory implementation model has contributed to the program’s success in supporting over 2 million family farms.

Implementation participation might involve serving on local committees, partnering with government agencies to deliver services, or providing ongoing feedback about how programs are working on the ground.

### Monitoring: keeping policies on track

Monitoring is like having a GPS for policy implementation—it helps ensure you’re heading in the right direction and alerts you when course corrections are needed. When farmers and stakeholders participate in monitoring, they become the eyes and ears of policy effectiveness.

This might involve tracking whether subsidies actually reach small farmers, monitoring if new regulations are helping or hindering agricultural productivity, or documenting unintended consequences of policy changes. Participatory monitoring often reveals issues that government officials might miss from their offices.

In India’s National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, community-based monitoring by farmer groups and civil society organizations has been crucial in identifying corruption, ensuring wage payments, and improving program delivery. These monitors conduct regular audits, verify work completion, and report issues directly to higher authorities.

### Evaluation: learning from experience

The evaluation stage is where stakeholders help determine whether policies achieved their intended goals and what lessons can be learned for future policy-making. This isn’t just about measuring success or failure—it’s about understanding why certain approaches worked or didn’t work, and how they can be improved.

Participatory evaluation goes beyond statistics and surveys. It involves gathering stories, understanding impacts on different groups, and identifying both intended and unintended consequences. When farmers participate in evaluation, they can explain not just what happened, but why it happened and what it means for their communities.

## Who should have a seat at the table?

Effective agricultural policy participation requires bringing together diverse voices that represent the full spectrum of agricultural stakeholders. This diversity ensures that policies don’t inadvertently favor one group while disadvantaging others.

**Small-scale farmers** often face unique challenges related to limited resources, market access, and vulnerability to economic shocks. Their participation ensures policies address the needs of the agricultural majority in many developing countries.

**Commercial farmers** bring perspectives on market dynamics, technological innovation, and large-scale production challenges. Their input is crucial for policies affecting agricultural competitiveness and export markets.

**Women farmers** face distinct challenges related to land ownership, access to credit, and cultural barriers. Their participation is essential because they often have different priorities and face different constraints than male farmers.

**Youth in agriculture** represent the future of farming and often have innovative ideas about technology adoption, sustainable practices, and market opportunities. However, they’re frequently underrepresented in policy discussions.

**Agricultural cooperatives and farmer organizations** can represent collective interests and have the organizational capacity to engage effectively in policy processes.

**Researchers and extension workers** provide technical expertise and can help translate between farmer experiences and policy requirements.

**Agribusiness companies** understand market dynamics, supply chain challenges, and the practical implications of regulatory changes.

## Making participation meaningful, not just ceremonial

True participation goes beyond token consultation. It requires creating genuine opportunities for stakeholders to influence decisions, not just rubber-stamp predetermined conclusions.

Meaningful participation starts with timing—involving stakeholders early enough that their input can actually shape policy direction, not just minor details. It also requires providing stakeholders with the information and capacity they need to participate effectively.

For example, if farmers are asked to comment on a complex trade policy, they need to understand how trade agreements might affect local markets, input costs, and export opportunities. This might require translation into local languages, simplified explanations of technical concepts, or training workshops to build stakeholder capacity.

Meaningful participation also requires feedback loops—letting stakeholders know how their input was used and why certain suggestions were or weren’t incorporated into final policies. This builds trust and encourages continued engagement.

## Overcoming common barriers to participation

Despite the clear benefits of participatory approaches, several barriers often prevent effective stakeholder engagement in agricultural policy processes.

**Geographic barriers** can exclude rural farmers who live far from policy meetings or consultation venues. Solutions might include holding consultations in rural areas, using mobile technology for remote participation, or supporting farmer organizations to represent broader constituencies.

**Language and literacy barriers** can prevent effective participation when policy documents are written in technical language or languages that stakeholders don’t speak fluently. Addressing this requires translation, visual communication tools, and simplified explanations of complex concepts.

**Time and resource constraints** mean that farmers might not be able to attend lengthy consultation processes, especially during busy agricultural seasons. Flexible scheduling, compensation for participation time, and efficient meeting formats can help address these constraints.

**Power imbalances** between government officials, large agribusiness, and small farmers can inhibit honest dialogue. Creating safe spaces for discussion, using neutral facilitators, and establishing clear ground rules for participation can help level the playing field.

## Success stories from around the world

Vietnam’s agricultural policy development process provides an inspiring example of effective stakeholder participation. When developing its National Target Program for New Rural Development, the government conducted extensive consultations with farmers, cooperatives, and local officials. This participatory approach helped identify locally relevant development priorities and ensured strong community ownership of the program.

In Mexico, the Sustainable Modernization of Traditional Agriculture Program (MasAgro) involves farmers as co-researchers in developing improved crop varieties and farming practices. Farmers don’t just adopt new technologies—they help test, adapt, and improve them based on local conditions and needs.

These success stories share common elements: genuine commitment to stakeholder input, adequate resources for participation processes, clear mechanisms for incorporating feedback, and ongoing dialogue rather than one-time consultations.

## Building the future of agricultural policy together

As agricultural systems become increasingly complex and interconnected, participatory approaches to policy-making become not just beneficial, but essential. Climate change, globalization, and technological innovation create challenges that require the combined wisdom of farmers, researchers, policymakers, and other stakeholders.

The future of agricultural policy participation lies in leveraging new technologies to facilitate broader and more efficient engagement. Digital platforms can enable remote participation, mobile apps can facilitate real-time feedback, and social media can help build networks of engaged stakeholders.

However, technology should enhance rather than replace face-to-face dialogue and community-based participation processes. The most effective approaches will likely combine high-tech tools with traditional consultation methods, ensuring that all stakeholders can participate regardless of their access to technology.

**What do you think?** How can your local agricultural community better engage in policy processes, and what barriers might need to be overcome to ensure all farmers have a voice in shaping the policies that affect their livelihoods?

--- SOURCE 3: Community Agriculture: Concepts, Models, and Impacts | USU ---
URL: https://extension.usu.edu/sustainability/research/community-agriculture-concepts-models-impacts

SUMMARY:
Community agriculture aims to increase civic participation in personal food production, while improving both diets and food security. Individuals and households

FULL CONTENT:
![Utah State University logo](https://www.usu.edu/assets/bs4/images/logos/utah-state-logo.svg)

## Search Utah State University:

![UState logo](https://www.usu.edu/assets/bs4/images/logos/u-state-logo.svg)

# Community Agriculture: Concepts, Models, and Impacts

Utah State University Department of Environment and Society and Utah Conservation Corps

[Download Fact Sheet](/sustainability/files/community-ag-FINAL.pdf)

## Defining Community Agriculture

Community agriculture initiatives are often run by organizations (1) relying on volunteer structures; (2) growing produce sustainably; and (3) aiming to improve health and access to food in their communities.![Farming](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-garden.jpg)

![Farming](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-garden.jpg)

To clarify “growing produce sustainably,” the Utah Conservation Corps Urban Community Farm (UCCUCF) (a volunteer and AmeriCorps-driven community agriculture model based in Logan, Utah) engages in the following regenerative agriculture practices:

## Community Agriculture vs. Community-Supported Agriculture

![Workers in garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-garden-2.jpg)Community agriculture is similar to community-supported agriculture (CSA) in that they both provide local food to their communities. Where they differ is in their approach. Community-supported agriculture models are structured in a way that community members pay an amount upfront that covers a weekly produce delivery for the entire season (McMurray et al., 2017). If some of the produce fails, the shareholders have already paid for the risks. Community agriculture works with local farmers, gardeners, and volunteers to donate produce. Using the UCC UCF as an example, participants and volunteers are trained to grow produce with the intention of donating to communities struggling with food security.

![Workers in garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-garden-2.jpg)

## Community Agriculture Growth During Times of Hardship

During the major world wars, millions of Americans transitioned front and backyards, disused space at schools, and vacant lots into “victory” gardens to help with the war efforts. During this period, it is estimated from promotional materials that 15 million victory gardens produced 7.5 billion pounds of food and provided 40% of the nation’s vegetable needs (Lawson, 2005).

Similar to past times of hardship, the COVID-19 pandemic’s disruption of the food supply chain, increas in the physical and economic barriers that restrict access to food, and catastrophic rise in food waste because of labor shortages have brought to the forefront the need to increase food security through local production in and around urban centers (Lal, 2020). Home gardening and urban community agriculture models (occurring at a micro and household scale) are one way to help reduce our dependence on imported fruits and vegetables, thus increasing food security (Tagtow & Richey, 2012). As suggested by Lal (2020), home and community gardening “can play an important role in advancing food and nutritional security during and after the COVID-19 pandemic, while also strengthening the provisioning of numerous ecosystem services (i.e., plant biodiversity, microclimate, water runoff, water quality, human health)” (p.871). This is especially important in Utah, as we import 98% of our fresh vegetables and 96% of our fresh fruit (Teichert, 2018; Envision Utah, n.d.).

Urban agriculture is producing almost 20% of the world’s food supply and can play an even more critical role in achieving food security during a global crisis such as that created by COVID-19 (Lal, 2020). Although local food production is seeing a resurgence under pandemic conditions, many planners do not incorporate community agriculture into urban planning as they lack the connections and knowledge to implement these programs (Mendes et al., 2008). As a result, calls for support by community agriculture advocates to local government and even community development corporations are often neglected (Campbell, 2004). In an effort to help overcome the barriers to increasing food production, this publication uses the UCC UCF as a model for community agriculture.

Included within are:

## Community Agriculture Benefits

**“The people who use neighborhood gardens vary as much as the plants that are grown in them.”**

**-Laura Lawson**

### Health

Community agriculture can increase access to healthy foods like fruits and vegetables, especially in low-income areas with limited access otherwise. It also provides opportunities for public health programming to improve nutritional knowledge and attitudes (Hodgson et al., 2011). Community agriculture aims to increase civic participation in personal food production, while improving both diets and food security. Individuals and households who participate in such programs are over three times more likely to consume fruits and vegetables at least five times a day than people without a gardening household member (Alaimo et al., 2008), leading to improved diets and healthier lifestyles.

In a thematic review, Dyg, Christensen, and Peterson (2020) found that community garden participation is positively associated with reducing body weight and hypertension, and increasing physical activity and knowledge. On a psychological level, gardens offer minorities a place to connect to their cultural heritage by providing an area to express their ethnic identity and grow specialty food items not available elsewhere. This can lead to an enhanced sense of well-being (Milburn & Vail, 2010).

### Social

![Four people in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-garden-3.jpg)Community agriculture provides many social benefits, including opportunities for community engagement, fostering interactions between diverse communities, as well as health and environmental stewardship education (Dyg et al., 2020; Lawson, 2005). Building connections between growers and consumers can also play an important role in supporting economic security. Because the community gardens within community agriculture models offer many benefits and consider barriers to food access, they provide a venue to link the concerns of urban planners, farmers, food banks, community development corporations, social service agencies, and environmentalists (Lawson, 2005). In addition, community gardens positively influence selfesteem, independence, and personal control, particularly for refugees (Dyg et al., 2020).

![Four people in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-garden-3.jpg)

Other social benefits include creating a safe community space and introducing new friendships that render a collective investment in the well-being of the neighborhood (Hodgson et al., 2011). In areas like New York, St. Louis, Seattle, or Los Angeles, neighborhood gardens demonstrate high cultural diversity of their participants. This can be attributed to immigration patterns that are reflected in the design and planting of community gardens (Lawson, 2005). These community programs also create pathways for local educators, university faculty, and local organizations to share knowledge with the community (Feenstra, 2002).

### Economic

A key economic benefit is the reduction in grocery expenses when receiving harvest from community gardens; a conservative estimate is that a family can save approximately $475 a year (Patel 1991). Community agriculture can also increase property values, local employment opportunities, and income (Hodgson et al., 2011). These projects often train novice gardeners and lead to increased employment in food production, manufacturing, and distribution. Kobayashi, Tyson, and Abi-Nader (2010) estimate more than 35,000 farmers and gardeners have emerged from civic/community agriculture projects. On a larger scale, local governments could benefit from investing in community agriculture because the costs of maintenance and development are typically less than those of traditional city parks (Milburn & Vail, 2010).

### Environmental

Community agriculture can help foster environmental connectedness, political consciousness, and activism among youth and adults (Spilkova, 2017). Additionally, it can decrease stormwater runoff and air pollution, and increase biodiversity and species habitat. This model is also a great use of vacant property to prevent harm to the area via crime or trash accumulation (Hodgson et al., 2011) and reintroduce green spaces and nature to urban areas (Lawson, 2005).

## Community Agriculture Challenges

Common challenges experienced during planning and development include location (i.e., adequate sunlight), access to water (i.e., no competition with neighboring properties for water resources), and finding qualified/educated leadership that can handle unforeseen problems (Milburn & Vail, 2010). These programs vary from place to place and are difficult to replicate unless a model from a similar area exists. For example, California has a nearly year-round growing season where many other areas of the country do not. Other factors involved are climate change, local politics, land availability, and funding. The majority of these factors will differ in each locale (Lawson, 2005).

### Risks

Community agriculture also presents potential health and environmental risks. Some factors to examine include the history of the locations (past uses) and proximity to other 3 industrial sites, automobile traffic, and other pollutants. Consider soil and water contaminants stemming from industrial wastes and pollutants, such as heavy metals, hydrocarbons, acids or bases, asbestos, solvents, or pathogenic organisms. Assessment of prior cleanup or containment of a site is extremely important as it can present serious health risks to both producers and consumers through contact with contaminated water and soil. These issues can also lead to the contaminants of foods that may also be a risk if consumed (Hodgson et al., 2011).

Inappropriately managed compost facilities, use of fertilizers and pesticides, and poor cultivation, harvesting, poultry, and animal-keeping practices can lead to noise and odor nuisances. Regulatory structures can help ensure that urban agriculture complements other land uses. Limited land tenure, access to water, and inadequate funding can also present significant challenges and risks to the success of community agriculture (Hodgson et al., 2011).

## The Utah Conservation Corps Urban Community Farm (UCC UCF): A Community Agriculture Model

### History and Mission

The UCC UCF is a nearly year-round, four-season, civic engagement and agriculture education project that started in 2017 in Logan, Utah. The UCC UCF aims to improve food security by increasing civic participation in crop production and by providing a range of education, training, and other resources to the local food-insecure population. Gardeners learn how to maintain efficient and productive small-scale agricultural systems, thereby strengthening individual self-reliance and access to fresh food items, which contributes to the overall strengthening of food security. All crop yields are donated to the local foodinsecure community.

The UCC UCF partners with Utah State University personnel to develop its program capacity resources (program tracking tools, education and training materials, outreach strategies), improve the project’s visibility within the community, and maximize community impact. This partnership helps the UCC UCF to publicly illustrate how small-scale seasonal production systems provide consistent crop yields year-round and fosters increased participation in civic agriculture.

The UCC UCF documented the following accomplishments in 2019:

Currently, the UCC UCF is staffed by the manager, two AmeriCorps Volunteers in Service to America (VISTAs), and four summer associate VISTA members. The UCC UCF manager is in charge of the daily operations and directly supervises volunteers. The two AmeriCorps VISTAs provide indirect service support, including volunteer recruitment and management as well as grant writing and resource development. Summer associate VISTA members complete direct service in the summer growing season. Crops are chosen based on past and anticipated user demand.

![Two friends in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-uccgarden-4.jpg)Volunteers are heavily relied on for this program, so training and education is an important factor. UCC UCF participants are taught how to propagate plants using methods and techniques that conserve natural resources and that are replicable in household and small-scale farming systems. Other educational opportunities for youth and adults via weekly activities and bi-monthly seasonal workshops include cultivation, management propagation techniques, seasonal and environmental factors that influence propagation, plant health management, use and management of the extended season structures, and harvest techniques. The primary target audience is low-income, food-insecure individuals, and individuals inactive in urban agriculture.

![Two friends in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-uccgarden-4.jpg)

The amount of produce UCC UCF grows and harvests is measured by weight. In future growing seasons, they plan also to evaluate impact through:

## Additional Successful Community Agriculture Models

**Alemany Farm** [(alemanyfarm.org/)](http://alemanyfarm.org/) is a 4.5-acre hybrid urban farm in southeastern San Francisco. Their main goals are fighting food insecurity, and providing environmental education by using different techniques including workshops, educational courses, a free neighborhood produce-delivery program, and field trips.

**The Community Action Coalition of South-Central Wisconsin** ([cacscw.org/](http://cacscw.org/)) is a nonprofit anti-poverty organization supporting many low-income families by growing food in more than 50 community gardens throughout the city. Their efforts have provided nearly 2,000 low-income households with access to fresh fruits and vegetables, reducing their grocery bills.

**![Four people in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-vista-5.jpg)DeLaney Community Farm** ([dug.org/about-delaneycommunity-farm/](http://dug.org/about-delaneycommunity-farm)) is a project of Denver Urban Gardens located in Aurora, Colorado, and was created to increase access to fresh fruits and vegetables while also providing opportunities for nutrition education in low-income areas. Courses offered focus on all aspects of food production and consumption, such as growing, preparing, cooking, tasting, and sharing.

![Four people in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-vista-5.jpg)

**The Resource Center** ([cityfarmchicago.org/](http://cityfarmchicago.org/)) is a nonprofit organization in Chicago that operates a City Farm and has an agreement with the city to use vacant, publicly owned land, with the understanding that it may need to relocate every couple of years. The City Farm has had to relocate four times in the past 25 years but has been exceptionally successful regardless.

**![five people in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-6.jpg)The P-Patch Community Gardening Program** ([seattle.gov/neighborhoods/programs-and-services/p-patchcommunity-gardening](/sustainability/research/seattle.gov/neighborhoods/programs-and-services/p-patchcommunity-gardening)) is located in Seattle, Washington. This program is managed by Seattle’s Department of Neighborhoods and now has about 90 community gardens. They partner with many organizations, which contributes to the great success of the program. One of their main goals is providing produce for low-income populations through their Market Garden Program. It encourages low-income families to grow and sell produce to improve their situations.

![five people in a garden](/sustainability/images/community-agriculture-6.jpg)

All photos by Elizabeth Sodja, Gateway & Natural Amenity Region (GNAR) Initiative Coordinator

## Glossary

**AmeriCorps**: A network of national service programs working to improve lives and foster civic engagement.

**Community agriculture**: Often run by organizations who are growing produce sustainably with the goal to improve health and access to food in their communities, especially among underrepresented populations.

**Community-supported agriculture (CSA)**: Provides a direct connection between local community members and growers, generally through purchasing a weekly “share” of locally grown goods (McMurray et al., 2017).

**Local food:** Often sold directly from growers to consumers through farmers markets, roadside stands, and CSAs (Hand et al., 2018).

**Regenerative agriculture**: Farming and grazing practices that, among other benefits, mitigate climate change by rebuilding soil organic matter and restoring degraded soil biodiversity, resulting in both carbon drawdown and water cycle improvements (Regeneration International, 2019).

**Urban agriculture**: Can include community and private gardens, edible landscaping, fruit trees, food-producing green roofs, aquaculture, farmers markets, small-scale farming, hobby beekeeping, and food composting (Mendes et al., 2008).

# Get Involved

Agricultural producers like you are the backbone of what we do. You’re not only who we serve, but who we learn from. We rely on your expertise to help us develop programs and offer services that best suit local needs. Your relationships with other producers in your community help us expand our reach to ensure all farmers, ranchers and forest landowners have access to available USDA programs.

## On This Page

## Get Involved in USDA Program Delivery

![Icon of a farm](/sites/default/files/2021-06/farm-icon.svg)

![Icon of a farm](/sites/default/files/2021-06/farm-icon.svg)

### Farm Service Agency County Committees

Farm Service Agency (FSA) county committees serve as a direct link between the agricultural community and the USDA. Committee members help deliver FSA farm programs at the local level and make FSA programs serve the needs of local producers.

Each June, FSA seeks nominations for producers to serve on the committee. Voting takes from early November to early December. Newly elected members begin their term in January of the following year. Members serve a three-year term.

FSA also has county committees specifically focused on urban agriculture. These committees work to encourage and promote urban, indoor and other emergency agricultural practices. Additionally, they may address other issues including food access, community engagement, and more.

[Learn More About FSA County Committees](https://www.fsa.usda.gov/news-room/county-committee-elections/index)

![Icon of river and mountains](/sites/default/files/2021-06/conservation-icon.svg)

![Icon of river and mountains](/sites/default/files/2021-06/conservation-icon.svg)

### Conservation Districts

Almost every county has an organization dedicated to conserving and promoting healthy soils, water, forests and wildlife. These districts work closely with the staff at the local Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) to coordinate assistance from public and private sources from the local to the federal levels, to develop solutions for local natural resource concerns.

Districts are often governed by an elected board of supervisors or commissioners. Elections vary from state to state. Many district boards also include opportunities to serve as assistant commissioners or supervisors.

[Contact Your Local Conservation District](https://www.nacdnet.org/general-resources/conservation-district-directory/)

## USDA Advisory Committees

There are many leadership opportunities available to you beyond the leadership opportunities available to you beyond the local level. USDA has [over 40 advisory committees](https://www.usda.gov/about-usda/general-information/staff-offices/white-house-liaison-office/advisory-committees). These committees advise the Secretary on a variety of topics.

## Connect and Learn with Partners

### Underlined Header Cooperative Extension Offices

USDA and agricultural colleges in the Land Grant University System around the country together support an extensive network of state, regional, and county cooperative extension offices. These offices address common issues faced by agricultural producers, and conduct workshops and educational events for the agricultural community.

[Visit a Cooperative Extension Office Near You](https://nifa.usda.gov/extension)

### Underlined Header Farmland Information Center

Farmland Information Center is a clearinghouse for information about farmland protection and stewardship. The Farm Information center is a project of American Farmland Trust maintained on behalf of and with support from USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service. Through the [farmland protection directory](http://www.farmlandinfo.org/directory), producers can search geographically for public programs and land trusts that protect farm and ranch land for agriculture.

[Search Farmland Information Center](http://www.farmlandinfo.org/)

### Underlined Header Your State Department of Agriculture

The National Association of State Departments of Agriculture’s (NASDA) website contains contact information plus an ag snapshot of statistics for your state. NASDA Members are coregulators with the federal government on a host of responsibilities including animal health, farmland protection, food safety, grain regulation, pesticide registration, and more.  

[Visit the NASDA Website](https://www.nasda.org/states/state-directory)

## Lead Locally

### Underlined Header Research and Promotion Program Boards

Be a voice for your industry by serving on a Research and Promotion Program Board. These programs, which are overseen by USDA Agricultural Marketing Service, focus on research, marketing, and consumer outreach efforts that improve, maintain, and develop opportunities for agricultural commodities. There are 22 Research and Promotion programs serving a variety of commodity industries, from eggs to soybeans, lumber to lamb.

[Serve on a Research and Promotion Program Board](http://www.ams.usda.gov/rules-regulations/research-promotion)

### Underlined Header 4-H

4-H partners with universities to provide mentoring and help youth build a foundation of leadership and skills for success.

[Learn More About 4-H](https://4-h.org/parents/ways-to-participate/4-h-camps/)

### Underlined Header FFA

The National FFA Organization develops the potential of students for premier leadership, personal growth, and career success as an integral part of agricultural education—from production farming, agribusiness, and forestry to biotechnology, marketing, and food processing.

[Learn More About the FFA](https://www.ffa.org/)

## Find a Mentor

SCORE offers free mentoring, workshops, and online resources for entrepreneurs, small business owners, farmers, ranchers, and agribusiness. USDA and its partners across rural America are working with SCORE to support new farming and ranching operations.

[Learn More About Finding or Becoming a Mentor](https://www.score.org/usda)

## Find Your Local Service Center

[USDA Service Centers](/node/28855) are locations where you can connect with Farm Service Agency, Natural Resources Conservation Service, or Rural Development employees for your business needs. Enter your state and county below to ﬁnd your local service center and agency offices. If this locator does not work in your browser, please visit [offices.usda.gov.](https://offices.usda.gov)

Learn more about our [Urban Service Centers](/node/29573).  
  
Visit the Risk Management Agency website to ﬁnd a [regional or compliance office](https://www.rma.usda.gov/) or to ﬁnd an [insurance agent](https://prodwebnlb.rma.usda.gov/apps/AgentLocator/#/) near you.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Engaging communities in agricultural policy ensures that local needs and perspectives are prioritized, fostering a sense of ownership and accountability in food security initiatives.
Collaboration among farmers, NGOs, and government entities enhances resource sharing and knowledge exchange, creating a more robust support system for sustainable agricultural practices.
Involving diverse stakeholders, including marginalized groups, guarantees that policies reflect the realities of all community members, ensuring equitable access to resources and opportunities.
Establishing effective feedback loops allows stakeholders to voice their concerns and experiences, facilitating continuous improvement in policy implementation and increasing trust in governance.
